title: Image Generation
summary: This podcast discusses the place of image generation and its diversion in the creative process.
showNotes: Part of the Supernova podcast series where Master of Media Design students investigate algorithms embedded in our everyday lives.
transcript:
Julien Tavelli : « We have to find a way to test it to push the boundaries and with the first Dall-E, there was not so much possibilities to enter into the technology »

Naomi Blidari « Sometimes you prompt one thing, you regenerate and you finish with something totally different but you like it anyway »

If aesthetic is seen as a fundamentally human domain, we speak today about generative synesthesia or artificial augmented reality as the interaction between artificial intelligence and human creativity.

In recent years, AI harmonizes human exploration and allows exploitation, fostering new creative workflows that could apply to any art field. However, for some it seems like the new best tool in the creative process that we should see as a revolution. For others there is skepticism about their usefulness and legitimacy in art and design practices. If we look back, those tools seem to push creativity much like the 19th century, when photography would defy art and spread avant-garde movements—initially mimicking painting; photography, like AI today, offers a faster transformative tool for artists.

From an academic paper called Artificial Augmented Creativity by ETH Zurich, generative AI echoes surrealist practices by embracing chain automation and the subconscious, unlocking dream-like creation that challenges creativity and invites chaos within order.

Like surrealism, AI questions traditional authorship by prioritizing ideas over execution and operating beyond human control, akin to the subconscious. Some view generative AI as both a revival of art history currents and a gateway to a new era where AI acts as a creative partner, enabling unprecedented forms of art.

In this podcast I invited two people from respectively different design fields. They are concerned by the legitimacy of those new tools but also their utility in their process.

JT « It's still a bit vague to me, I don't see right now an application really where I could use it really properly when I can really manage to get a control of—or even uncontrolled but that is surprising me enough. »

NB « When you are searching for something specific everyone finishes by having the same images » Generative AI is currently somewhat of a monopoly, with only a few organisations controlling a handful of AI systems; there is a risk of artistic content generation operating as a recursive loop, with the outputs generated by AI systems feeding back into those same models at scale.

When abstract pictures are painted, built or created, it is precisely because it is not possible to reason verbally. In art and design, there are non-verbal rules of composition and construction, and we are not constantly anticipating intentions and materialising a language.

For instance, it is shown that contemporary AIs are not replacing illustrators, but rather a preconceived idea of illustration.

NB « Sometimes the final image that you have is totally different from the prompt that you had at the beginning »

For some reason, the idea of hijacking those AI tools has become more and more a new way of apprehending it. Indeed it seems an interesting way of proceeding would be to play with the extent and location of the chance introduced into phases of production.

JT « I think when you have a tool, if you want really to push it to use it, you will somehow need to hack it or to know it very well to be able to go into the code or to manage the tool to make what you want to do and to destruct the tool »

From the book « Design sous artifice » by Anthony Masure, the fascination lies in dissecting an optimized model structure and creatively hijacking its process, re-directing, blending or reinjecting new information to generate unexpected results.

By challenging database limits and embracing accidents, designers transform technical, historical and cultural objects, pushing models beyond their intended use and unlocking new forms of creativity.

In order to conclude this podcast, I wanted to share with you two artistic projects pushing the boundaries of AI image generation while breaking free from conventional uses:

Both of those projects challenge AI's reliance on structured processes, emphasizing unexpected outcomes. They use ambiguous prompts to turn AI misinterpretations, randomness and error into creative expression. By embracing those AI "failures," both projects reject polished outputs, using the medium to explore limits and vulnerabilities.

Together, they embody a surrealist ethos of subversion and randomness, challenging AI's conventional uses while transforming it into a medium for chaotic and provocative creativity.

Thank you for listening to this podcast and see you soon
